Services

What we offer

01

Video Editing

From raw footage to a finished story. Every cut is made with intention, shaping the pacing, rhythm, and emotion.

02

Cinematography

Light, framing, and movement that speak on their own. Every shot is built to feel right, not just look good.

03

Animation

From title sequences to full pieces. Ideas brought to life through motion, timing, and detail.

04

Visual Effects

Compositing, CGI, and visual work that blends naturally into the frame, using tools like Nuke and Adobe After Effects.

About

Who we are

We believe every frame is a chance to say something true.

Jeon Films is a creative studio founded by Jivan, originally from Nepal and now based in Utah, United States. With over five years of hands-on experience in visual production, we bring craft, intention and a cross-cultural perspective to every project we take on.

We work across video editing, cinematography, animation and visual effects. From short films and commercials to branded content and client productions, we always lead with the story and let the craft follow.

Whether we are shaping raw footage or starting from nothing, we give it everything we have.

Final Portfolio FILM 3500 · Spring 2026

JIVAN CHAUDHARY.

Film Theory Portfolio. FILM 3500, Spring 2026.

Contents
  1. 01Reflection — A Semester in Film Theory
  2. 02Process Work I — Black Spectatorship
  3. 03Process Work II — Theorizing Parasite
§ 01 — Reflection

A Semester in Film Theory

When I started FILM 3500, I thought I already knew how to look at movies. I had been editing, shooting, and doing VFX work for years back in Nepal before I came to the U.S. I figured theory would just give me fancier words for things I was already doing on set. I was wrong. What this class actually gave me was a different way of paying attention — a way of watching that keeps asking who a film is built for, who it leaves out, and what the craft itself is saying even when nobody is saying anything out loud.

The hardest part of the semester for me was the group presentation on Manthia Diawara's "Black Spectatorship." It was hard in two ways. First, the reading itself. Diawara's idea of the "resisting spectator" is that identification in Hollywood is never neutral. Race shapes who gets to step into a film comfortably and who has to push back against it. Reading him next to Mulvey and hooks made me go back and rethink scenes I had loved for years without ever questioning them. Second, the presenting part. Speaking in front of a group is genuinely hard for me. I prepared a lot. I knew the Diawara reading well and I had my notes on the "Gus chase" sequence from Birth of a Nation ready to go. But when the moment came, I started getting anxious and hesitating, and none of that had anything to do with whether I understood the material.

The breakthrough came later, while I was writing Essay 2 on Parasite. I was using John Thornton Caldwell's production culture framework, and somewhere in the middle of drafting, something clicked. Parasite is a film about invisible labor and hidden suffering. But the most important set in the film, the Park family's house, was built from scratch on an empty lot so it would feel like it had always been there. Lee Ha Jun, the production designer, kept that secret until after Cannes. That stopped me. The film's critique of capitalism was produced through the same kind of invisible, below-the-line craft that the film is criticizing. Once I saw that, I couldn't unsee it.

What I am taking with me from this class, into my own editing and VFX work, is that craft choices are never only technical. The way a space is built, lit, cut, and sounded is always making an argument, whether we mean it to or not. That is the lesson I was not expecting to learn here, and it is the one I think will stay with me the longest.

§ 02 — Process Work I

Group Presentation on Black Spectatorship

Week
11 — Theorizing Social Differences
Reading
Manthia Diawara, "Black Spectatorship" (Screen, 1988)
Format
Group presentation with slides
Films
Birth of a Nation (1915), The Color Purple (1985), 48 Hours (1982)

This group presentation was built around Manthia Diawara's essay on the "resisting spectator." His argument is that Black viewers of classical Hollywood cinema often refuse the identification the film is asking for, because the dominant reading of the text is built on racist assumptions they cannot accept. Our presentation walked through Diawara's reading of the "Gus chase" sequence in D.W. Griffith's Birth of a Nation, and looked at how his argument carries into later films like The Color Purple and 48 Hours.

This was the most difficult piece of work I did this semester. The reading asked me to go back and rethink films I had loved without ever questioning them. Working through Diawara gave me a foundation I came back to when writing my final essay.

Open in Google Slides →
§ 03 — Process Work II

Final Essay — Theorizing Parasite

Assignment
Essay 2 — Theorizing the Media Object
Subject
Bong Joon-ho, Parasite (2019)
Frameworks
Production culture (Caldwell), auteur theory, transnational cinema
Builds From
Essay 1 — Formal Analysis of the same sequence

My final essay looks at the basement reveal scene in Bong Joon-ho's Parasite through three frameworks: production culture theory, auteur theory, and transnational cinema. My argument is that Parasite folds its critique of capitalism into the actual conditions of its own making.

The essay looks at how the Park family's house was built from scratch on an empty lot, how cinematographer Hong Kyung-pyo used large format cameras to make a manufactured space feel permanent, how Bong's recurring use of hidden places ties Parasite back to earlier films, and how the Hallyu wave made it possible for a film this specifically Korean to reach a global audience.

Read the Essay →
Jivan Chaudhary · FILM 3500 · Spring 2026 Weber State University
